bibliographic.citation: Karl Breul, 'Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols,' Englische Studien: Organ für englische Philologie 14 (Leipzig: O. R. Reisland, 1890), 401–408.
bibliographic.container_title: Englische Studien: Organ für englische Philologie
bibliographic.title: Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols

entities.description: The immediate print source and complete article encoded by CME00121. Breul prints two direct 1882 transcriptions from Advocates' Library MS 19.3.1 with textual notes and extensive German linguistic, literary, biblical, and comparative commentary.
entities.label: Karl Breul, 'Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols' (1890)
notes: Breul says he transcribed both poems in 1882 together with Sir Gowther and the Boke of Curtesy.
He dates both probably to the fifteenth century but says their original dialects cannot be determined from rhyme.
He retains variable manuscript spelling while normalizing capitalization of proper names and line starts, distinguishing u/v, and joining compounds; every textual departure is italicized.

rights.basis: The article was first published in 1890, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Breul's 1890 scholarly edition and commentary

bibliographic.citation: William Barclay David Donald Turnbull, ed., The Visions of Tundale; together with Metrical Moralizations and Other Fragments of Early Poetry, Hitherto Inedited (Edinburgh: Thomas G. Stevenson, 1843), 139–143.
bibliographic.title: The Visions of Tundale; together with Metrical Moralizations and Other Fragments of Early Poetry, Hitherto Inedited

entities.description: An earlier independent edition from the same Advocates' Library manuscript, then designated Jac. V.7.27. Turnbull prints the Nativity/Epiphany carol on pp.139–140 and the Annunciation carol on pp.141–143.
entities.label: William Barclay David Donald Turnbull, The Visions of Tundale (1843)
notes: Turnbull describes the source as the Advocates' Library manuscript Jac. V.7.27, a small fifteenth-century quarto of 216 folios; this is the current NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1.
No dependence of Breul's 1890 transcription on Turnbull is asserted: Breul explicitly says he copied both poems from the manuscript in 1882.

rights.basis: The volume was first published in 1843, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works.
rights.component: Turnbull's 1843 scholarly edition

access.accessMethod: NLS manuscript catalog and reading-room request subject to collection conditions
access.format: Physical codex

access.accessMethod: Contact NLS for preservation-quality files, omitted modern blanks if required, and a shelfmark-specific reuse statement
access.format: Digital reproduction; terms and completeness to be quoted

entities.description: The direct source of both Breul transcriptions and Turnbull's earlier printings. The Nativity/Epiphany carol occupies current ff.59r–59v; the Annunciation carol occupies ff.89v–90v.

holding.notes: Acquired for the national collections in 1925.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.3.1

physicalDescription.extent: xii + 216 + xviii folios; target carols at current ff.59r–59v and 89v–90v
notes: The codex was written in the North Midlands mostly by Richard Heeg, with material by James Hawghton and additions in other hands; no target-carol hand is assigned here without passage-specific evidence.
Breul's historical fol.59vo ff. locus for the Nativity carol conflicts with current direct-image and DIMEV f.59r–59v evidence.

rights.basis: The anonymous texts are embodied in a manuscript copied about 1480 and predate modern copyright by centuries. Physical-access and image-reuse terms are separate.
rights.component: Underlying medieval carol texts embodied in Adv.MS.19.3.1; physical ownership and digital images excluded

access.accessMethod: NLS IIIF Presentation manifest and IIIF Image API
access.format: IIIF images and manifest

localCopies.label: Complete provider-published Adv.MS.19.3.1 IIIF bundle

workPortion.label: Two noncontiguous CME00121 carol portions in Adv.MS.19.3.1
locators: Nativity/Epiphany: current ff.59r–59v = IIIF canvases 133–134 = ZIP images/000133.jpg–images/000134.jpg
Annunciation: current ff.89v–90v = IIIF canvases 194–196 = ZIP images/000194.jpg–images/000196.jpg
notes: The start/end URLs delimit a discontiguous envelope. The exact Nativity endpoint is canvas 134 at https://dg-view.nls.uk/iiif/2/1341%2F6318%2F134163183.5/full/1600,/0/default.jpg; the exact Annunciation start is canvas 194 at https://dg-view.nls.uk/iiif/2/1341%2F6390%2F134163903.5/full/1600,/0/default.jpg.
Canvas 132 is a preceding-page control and canvas 135 is a following-page control for the Nativity carol; canvas 193 precedes and canvas 197 follows the Annunciation carol.
All 482 canvases exposed by the provider manifest were captured at a non-upscaling 1600-pixel-wide request. The provider says numerous modern blank flyleaves at the beginning were not all captured.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF item metadata links the Public Domain Mark 1.0 and states that the material is free of known copyright restrictions. NLS guidance says faithful digitizations retain the original's status and waives related digitization rights with CC0 where they subsist; the exact item remains marked PDM rather than licensed.
rights.component: NLS digital page images and item-level facsimile

entities.description: The complete 482-canvas object exposed by the NLS IIIF manifest, including provider-published covers and endmatter. The Nativity/Epiphany carol is at canvases 133–134 and the Annunciation carol at canvases 194–196. NLS notes that numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured.
entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of Adv.MS.19.3.1

rights.basis: The exact IIIF item metadata links the Public Domain Mark 1.0 and states that the material is free of known copyright restrictions. entities.description: An early-fifteenth-century English composite manuscript containing a four-stanza version of Ecce ancilla domini at f.182v. DIMEV says its first stanza has seven lines and the remaining three have eight; this is a collateral version, not Breul's direct source.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Add. 5943

physicalDescription.extent: iii parchment + 177 paper + iii parchment folios; Annunciation carol at f.182v under the manuscript's historical foliation
physicalDescription.format: Composite manuscript containing astronomical, moral, sermonic, administrative, and musical material
notes: DIAMM associates the codex with England and Hinton Priory in Somerset; this provenance is not converted into a work-composition claim.
The current public Cambridge finding-aid routes checked did not expose a complete facsimile. DIAMM lists images behind login, but their coverage and target-folio availability were not established.

entities.description: The Windsor Carol-book, probably written for St George's Chapel, Windsor, in 1430–1444. At ff.65v–66 it preserves a five-stanza musical version of As Holy Kirk Makes Mind with the burden 'Almyghty ihesu kynge of blysse' and a variant four-line burden structure.

entities.label: DIMEV v1.0 records 1173 and 589
notes: Record 1173 describes two witnesses: CUL Add. 5943 f.182v and NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1 ff.89v–90v.
Record 589 describes four witnesses: Bodley 77 ff.1v and 2, Egerton 3307 ff.65v–66, Harley 275 ff.146v–147, and NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1 f.59r–59v.
Record 589's Turnbull page value '13-40' conflicts with direct scan evidence for pp.139–140 and is preserved as an attributed data error.

rights.basis: The versioned release README and LICENSE identify Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International; no additional item-specific contractual restriction was found in the release.
rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data and project files

next_steps: Ask Cambridge whether DIAMM's login-gated Add. 5943 images include f.182v and whether a complete existing surrogate can be supplied.
Ask Bodleian Imaging Services whether complete masters exist for Bodley 77 and whether both sides of the binding strips can be reproduced safely.
Submit separate British Library enquiries for Egerton MS 3307 and Harley MS 275 and ask whether complete masters or legacy microfilms exist before commissioning new photography.
For every supplied object, record complete-object coverage, exact target boundaries, image rights, contractual terms, and fixity before caching.
openQuestions.question: Do complete public or restricted digital facsimiles exist for CUL Add. 5943, Bodley 77, Egerton MS 3307, or Harley MS 275 beyond the official routes checked on 2026-07-13?

next_steps: Ask NLS whether historical foliation, a recto/verso convention, or a simple Breul error explains the difference.
Consult Hardman 2000 and any cataloging history for foliation changes.
Continue to use current f.59r–59v and canvases 133–134 for access while preserving Breul's attributed fol.59vo ff. statement.
openQuestions.question: Why does Breul locate the Nativity/Epiphany carol at fol.59vo ff. when current NLS images and DIMEV place the complete text on f.59r–59v?

next_steps: Obtain complete images or reliable diplomatic transcriptions of all collateral witnesses.
Consult modern carol and musicological editions, including work on the Windsor Carol-book.
Do not add direct witness-to-witness edges until passage-level evidence supports them.

next_steps: Ask the University of Leeds whether an institutional ebook or licensed digital delivery exists.
Use ISBN 9780902296268 and OCLC 51223769 to check local and interlibrary-loan holdings.
Never substitute the cached two-page GBV title-and-contents preview for the complete facsimile.

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00121.xml; the U-M encoding, Breul and Turnbull editions, manuscript witnesses, facsimiles, and DIMEV descriptions are separate entities.
CME00121 encodes the complete Breul article, not the whole Englische Studien volume. The article contains two independent medieval works and two substantial German commentary sections.

The Annunciation poem has 56 medieval lines in seven eight-line stanzas. Two further L elements embedded in the second LG are Breul's Chaucer epigraph and are excluded from the historical poem metric.

Breul says the Nativity/Epiphany carol begins at fol.59vo ff.; DIMEV and direct current NLS image inspection instead establish f.59r–59v. Both attributed statements are preserved, and current foliation controls present-day access locators.
Breul's printed italics mark every departure from manuscript readings, including editorial supplies and normalization; XML HI/@REND='italic' is not evidence of manuscript emphasis.
DIMEV's Turnbull page range '13-40' for record 589 is a data error. Direct inspection of the complete 1843 scan confirms pp.139–140; the Annunciation carol follows on pp.141–143.
The NLS cache is complete relative to all 482 provider-published canvases, not every physical leaf: the provider says numerous modern blank opening flyleaves were not all captured.

The pinned CME root LICENSE uses Unlicense language framed as software. Its application to corpus XML/data/markup remains unresolved and is not broadened into a high-confidence repository-artifact rights claim.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00121, a complete encoding of Karl Breul's 1890 article 'Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols' on printed pp.401–408. Breul directly transcribed two independent medieval carols in 1882 from the manuscript now National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1: the Nativity/Epiphany carol at current ff.59r–59v and the Annunciation carol at ff.89v–90v. The complete 482-canvas NLS digital object, complete 496-page Englische Studien volume, and complete 1843 Turnbull volume are cached with fixity and exact work portions. Turnbull independently printed both NLS texts in 1843. DIMEV v1.0 records one collateral witness for the Annunciation carol and three for the Nativity carol; current official catalog and request routes are recorded, but no complete public facsimile was found for those four collateral manuscripts.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous poets; article by Karl Breul
catalogSummary.date: Medieval carols probably fifteenth century; earliest surviving witnesses early to mid-fifteenth century; both first printed in 1843; Breul article published 1890
catalogSummary.region: Original dialect and region unlocalized; Breul notes northern features in the Annunciation carol, while the shared NLS witness was copied in a North-Midland context
catalogSummary.title: Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols

catalogingSubject.label: Zwei mittelenglische Christmas Carols
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Karl Breul's complete 1890 scholarly article: two independent anonymous Middle English carols, their Latin refrains and alternating lines, and Breul's substantial German introductions, textual commentary, comparisons, source statement, and editorial-practice statement. It is not one medieval work, the whole journal volume, either manuscript as a physical object, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. The two carols and their manuscript traditions remain separately scoped parts.

contentParts.label: Ecce ancilla domini (Mariae Verkündigung)

locators.value: DIV2 N='1' TYPE='poem'; seven LG; 58 raw L

notes: The medieval poem has 56 lines. The XML's raw 58-L total includes a two-line Chaucer epigraph inserted inside LG N='2'; direct inspection of the print confirms that the two lines are editorial epigraph material, not part of the stanza.
Breul's seven footnotes align stanzas I–VI with Luke; one further textual footnote occurs at line 28.
contentParts.summary: A seven-stanza Annunciation song in which Gabriel announces the Incarnation and Mary repeatedly responds 'Ecce ancilla domini'; Breul aligns its central stanzas with Luke 1:26–38.

contentParts.label: As Holy Kirk Makes Mind / Intravit ventris thalamum

locators.value: DIV2 N='2' TYPE='poem'; ten LG; 38 L
ff.65v–66; five stanzas and variant four-line burden

notes: Breul historically locates this carol at fol.59vo ff., but the current NLS images and DIMEV place the complete NLS version on f.59r–59v. The attributed historical statement is preserved rather than silently normalized.
DIMEV's Turnbull bibliography gives pages '13-40'; direct inspection of the 1843 scan confirms pp.139–140 and shows that the DIMEV value dropped a 9.
contentParts.summary: A macaronic Nativity and Epiphany carol with a two-line burden and nine alternating Middle English and Latin quatrains on the Incarnation, Mary, Christ's birth, the star, and the gifts of the Magi.

contentParts.label: Breul's commentary on Mariae Verkündigung

locators.value: DIV2 N='1' TYPE='commentary'; 11 P, one Q, five nested LG, 22 quoted L

contentParts.summary: German commentary on the carol's relation to Luke, stanza form, repeated formulas, alliteration, vocabulary, northern linguistic features, textual cruxes, and the image of the knotted bond, including a five-stanza comparative quotation.

contentParts.label: Breul's commentary on Auf Weihnachten und Epiphanias

locators.value: DIV2 N='2' TYPE='commentary'; 21 P, three Q, 18 quoted L, CLOSER, DATELINE, and SIGNED

contentParts.summary: German commentary on macaronic religious song, the poem's Latin phrases and analogues, textual cruxes, the Magi's gifts, the manuscript source and loci, Breul's 1882 transcription, and his normalization and italic-emendation practices.

assertion.notes: Breul's broad dating is retained as a work estimate, separate from the dates of the early-fifteenth-century Cambridge and c.1480 NLS witnesses.
Breul's broad dating is retained as a work estimate, not inferred from one surviving copy.
CUL Add. 5943 preserves a four-stanza version at f.182v; the exact manuscript dating varies by cataloging source.

Egerton MS 3307 preserves a five-stanza musical version with a variant four-line burden and is securely dated 1430–1444. Bodley 77 is independently cataloged only as fifteenth century, so its binding-strip fragment could be earlier or contemporary; the broadened normalization avoids asserting that Egerton is certainly the first surviving copy.

dateStatements.display: Fifteenth century; earliest securely narrow witness range 1430–1444

assertion.notes: Turnbull's volume prints the Nativity/Epiphany carol on pp.139–140 and the Annunciation carol on pp.141–143. It is the earliest publication listed for either work in DIMEV v1.0.

scope.description: The NLS versions of both carols

assertion.notes: Breul dates his signed article Cambridge, August 1888; the journal volume was published in 1890.

extent.description: One DIV1 contains four DIV2 sections: two poem sections and two commentary sections. The verified textual total is 134 verse lines: 94 direct medieval carol lines and 40 verse-quotation lines in the commentary. The XML has 136 raw L elements because it embeds a two-line modern Chaucer epigraph inside the second Annunciation LG. The commentary sections additionally have 32 P, four Q, and five nested LG. Across the complete article are 12 NOTE, nine PB, 18 MILESTONE, 129 italic HI, one CLOSER, one DATELINE, and one SIGNED.

formStatements.description: The complete encoded article combines two verse works with substantial German prose commentary and embedded verse quotations.

assertion.notes: The XML places a two-line Chaucer epigraph inside the second Annunciation LG, so its raw 58 L elements exceed the 56 medieval poem lines.

formStatements.description: The first carol has seven eight-line stanzas; the second has a two-line burden and nine quatrains.

scope.description: Breul's transcriptions, textual notes, editorial interventions, and commentary
The Annunciation refrain, the alternating lines and burden of the Nativity/Epiphany carol, biblical citations, and comparative quotations

notes: The whole-unit form is mixed because the cataloged item is the complete article, not merely the two medieval poems.

Current NLS foliation and direct image boundaries control present-day locators; Breul's attributed historical folio statements are preserved in lineage notes, including the Nativity carol's discrepant fol.59vo fg.
Manuscript image and scan rights are not inferred from the public-domain status of the underlying medieval texts or nineteenth-century editions.

next_steps: Consult modern editions and linguistic studies of DIMEV records 1173 and 589.
Keep work composition, variant history, and each witness's copying date and place separate.
Do not infer an original North-Midland composition merely from the c.1480 NLS copy.
openQuestions.question: What composition dates and original dialect regions can current scholarship support for each carol beyond Breul's broad fifteenth-century estimate and limited linguistic observations?

next_steps: Obtain complete images or reliable diplomatic transcriptions of the four collateral witnesses.
Compare textual and burden variants without presuming direct copying between extant manuscripts.
Consult modern carol and musicological editions for version-specific analysis.
openQuestions.question: What stemmatic or performance relationships connect the NLS texts with the shorter Cambridge, Bodley, Egerton, and Harley variants?

next_steps: For cataloging and display, report 56 medieval Annunciation lines and identify the two extra L elements as editorial epigraph material.
Treat any XML structural correction as a separate source-repository change; do not silently alter the manifest count.
openQuestions.question: Should the two Chaucer epigraph lines currently encoded as L elements inside Annunciation stanza II be represented as explicit editorial paratext in future conversion or source cleanup?

assertion.notes: Breul identifies several northern spellings or inflectional features but explicitly says the original dialect cannot be determined from the rhymes.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous poet of Ecce ancilla domini
Anonymous poet of As Holy Kirk Makes Mind

scope.description: Authorship of the modern article prose, framing, and signed source/editorial statement; not authorship of either medieval carol
Breul says he transcribed both poems directly from the Advocates' Library manuscript in 1882.

subjects.label: Virgin Mary and the angel Gabriel

summaries.value: Karl Breul's complete 1890 article edits and comments on two anonymous fifteenth-century religious lyrics from the Heege Manuscript. Ecce ancilla domini retells the Annunciation in seven eight-line stanzas, closely following Luke 1:26–38 and ending each stanza with Mary's Latin response. As Holy Kirk Makes Mind is a macaronic Nativity and Epiphany carol whose two-line burden and nine quatrains alternate Middle English with Latin and culminate in the Magi and the symbolic meanings of their gifts.

CME00121 encodes the complete printed article on pp.401–408, including both poem texts, Breul's German commentary and comparative verse quotations, the final manuscript and editorial-practice statement, and the Cambridge, August 1888 dateline and signature. It does not encode the rest of Englische Studien volume 14.

The raw XML contains 136 L elements. Ninety-six are descendants of the two poem DIV2 elements, but two of those ninety-six are a Chaucer epigraph placed inside the second Annunciation LG rather than medieval carol lines. The direct edited carol total is therefore 94 lines: 56 for Ecce ancilla domini and 38 for As Holy Kirk Makes Mind. A further 40 L elements belong to verse quotations embedded in Breul's commentary.

Breul says he retained the manuscript's variable spelling but capitalized proper names and line openings, separated u from v, and joined compounds; every departure from the manuscript reading was printed in italics. CME00121 retains this typography as HI/@REND='italic', so italics indicate editorial difference, not manuscript emphasis. The file contains 129 italic HI elements, 96 within the poem divisions and 33 in commentary.

assertion.notes: This English display title translates Breul's German heading; it is not a second historical title-page form.
Breul's supplied German section title, not a manuscript rubric.
DIMEV's record name and the poem's repeated Latin refrain.

titles.value: Ecce ancilla domini / Seyd þo virgyn wythowtyn vice

assertion.notes: Breul's supplied German section title, not a manuscript rubric.
DIMEV's record name combines the Middle English and Latin opening lines of the stanza text.

titles.value: As Holy Kirk Makes Mind / Intravit ventris thalamum

scope.description: Two-line burden followed by the opening Middle English and Latin stanza lines

titles.value: The almyghty Kyng of blys / Assumpsit carnem virginis / As holy kyrke makys mynd / Intrauit ventris thalamum

work.author: Anonymous poets; article by Karl Breul
work.dateDisplay: Medieval carols probably fifteenth century; earliest surviving witnesses early to mid-fifteenth century; both first printed in 1843; Breul article published 1890
work.regionDisplay: Original dialect and region unlocalized; Breul notes northern features in the Annunciation carol, while the shared NLS witness was copied in a North-Midland context
